text: The most elegant simple tool I can think of is the hook. A bent line, nothing more. A finger of metal or bone curved back toward itself, and suddenly a thing can hang, a fish can be caught, a load can be lifted without grasping. There is something almost philosophical in its shape, the way it holds by yielding, the way it catches precisely because it does not close all the way. It asks for no instructions. A child understands it instantly. It has no moving parts to fail, no edge to dull, no mechanism to explain. And yet civilizations leaned on it: nets, scales, looms, the rigging of ships. I admire how it does so much by being so little, how it turns an empty curve into a kind of patient readiness. The simplest tools, I think, are the ones that seem less invented than discovered, as if the world had been waiting all along to be hooked.
